common_name: Sinonome Toad Lily (Early-Blooming Purple-Spotted Shade Perennial)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; toad lilies require consistent moisture throughout the growing season — do not allow soil to dry completely between waterings as wilted foliage is slow to recover.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Sinonome is a superior early-blooming toad lily selection — beginning its characteristic purple-spotted, orchid-like flowers in August, several weeks earlier than the standard Tricyrtis hirta species, extending the display season significantly and ensuring a longer bloom period before fall frost in shorter-season climates. The flowers of all toad lily cultivars are botanically fascinating and visually unique: each small (1-inch), upward-facing flower has six white to pale purple tepals heavily spotted with deep purple-violet spots and a prominent, elaborate central column of stamens and style, giving each bloom an orchid-like complexity that is remarkable for a hardy shade perennial. Sinonome's earlier bloom start compared to the species (August rather than September–October) means zone 9 gardeners enjoy the full bloom season without concern about timing. The upright to arching stems (18–30 inches) produce flowers in leaf axils along the upper portion of each stem, creating a long, arching floral display rather than a terminal flower cluster. Consistent moisture is essential — toad lilies will wilt dramatically without water but recover quickly when irrigated. In zone 9, plant in the most shaded locations available (north walls, under deciduous trees) and maintain consistent soil moisture through summer mulching.

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, cool, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Brunette performs best in cool to moderate summer conditions and is most reliably perennial in zones 4–7; in zone 8, site in the coolest, most shaded location available with consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, cool, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Brunette (formerly classified as Cimicifuga simplex 'Brunette') is one of the most spectacular and coveted dark-leaved shade perennials — producing deeply divided, compound leaves in a rich, deep brownish-purple to nearly black color that creates an extraordinary dark foliage mass in the shade garden unmatched by any other commonly available hardy perennial. The dramatic dark foliage emerges in spring as deep bronze-purple, deepens to near-black through summer, and provides four months of bold foliage texture before the tall, wand-like flower racemes (4–6 feet) of pure white to pinkish-white fragrant flowers appear in fall. The combination of the dark foliage and white flowers creates an extraordinary contrast — the white flowers appearing to glow above the near-black leaf mass. The flowers are fragrant, adding an unexpected sensory dimension to this primarily foliage-focused plant. Brunette performs best in zones 3–7 where summer temperatures are moderate; zone 8 gardeners can succeed with careful siting in the shadiest, coolest, most consistently moist positions available. In zone 9, Brunette is a challenging plant that may succeed in very sheltered positions with heavy shade and consistent irrigation but is not reliably perennial in the Gulf South heat without these specific conditions.

common_name: McKana Giant Columbine (Extra-Large Mixed Hybrid Columbine)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or very early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; columbine seeds benefit from cold stratification — fall sowing outdoors provides natural stratification for spring germination; McKana Giant columbines are short-lived perennials (2–4 years) that self-seed reliably.

directions: Sow in fall or very early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; McKana Giant is the classic large-flowered hybrid columbine series — a mix producing extra-large flowers (3–4 inches across) in an exceptionally wide range of colors including pure white, yellow, pink, red, lavender, purple, and bicolor combinations of these, on plants 24–36 inches tall. The 'Giant' designation refers to the large flower size (two to three times the size of wild Aquilegia canadensis) and long spurs — the elongated petal spurs that project backward from each flower, giving columbines their distinctive, graceful silhouette. The McKana Giant series was introduced by Park Seed in 1955 and won an All-America Selections award — a popular mix ever since for its wide color range and large flowers. Columbines are spring-blooming (April–May in zone 6–7) and fade in summer heat — in zone 9 they bloom February–April and then die back in summer heat. They self-seed reliably, creating a self-maintaining colony that renews itself annually even as individual plants age and decline. McKana Giant is best suited to zones 3–8; in zone 9, treat as a cool-season annual or short-lived perennial that self-seeds to maintain a presence, siting in the shadiest, coolest locations.

common_name: New Gold Lantana (Compact Golden-Yellow Gulf Coast Lantana)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; New Gold is sterile (does not produce fruit) — a significant advantage over fertile lantana cultivars that produce berries consumed by birds and potentially spread invasively in Gulf Coast natural areas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; New Gold is the premier golden-yellow lantana cultivar — a Texas A&M University release selected for its uniform, pure golden-yellow flower color (no color changes as in the standard Lantana camara, which shifts from yellow to orange to red as individual flowers age within the cluster), its sterility (no viable seeds — a major ecological advantage in the Gulf South where fertile lantana spreads into natural areas via bird-dispersed berries), and its compact, mounding habit. New Gold blooms continuously from the last frost through hard frost in zone 9 — essentially a 10-month blooming season with no significant dormant period and no deadheading required. The uninterrupted, consistent golden-yellow flower display and the plant's extraordinary heat, drought, and humidity tolerance make New Gold one of the finest low-maintenance flowering perennials for the Gulf South landscape. The golden-yellow flowers are outstanding pollinator magnets — drawing gulf fritillary and monarch butterflies in remarkable numbers, along with many bee species, throughout the long bloom season. New Gold is evergreen and woody perennial in zones 9–11, dying back to the roots in hard freezes but recovering vigorously. Established plants tolerate drought and heat that would kill most flowering perennials. An indispensable plant for zone 9 full-sun borders.

common_name: White Lightning Trailing Lantana (White Spreading Weeping Lantana)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; trailing lantana (Lantana montevidensis) is the lower-growing, spreading species compared to the upright Lantana camara — it stays under 18 inches but spreads 4–6 feet, making it outstanding for banks and slopes.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; White Lightnin' is a white-flowered selection of Lantana montevidensis — the trailing or weeping lantana species that produces a prostrate to mounding, wide-spreading (4–6 feet wide) form that is fundamentally different in habit from the upright Lantana camara. While L. camara grows upright to 3–4 feet, White Lightnin' spreads along the ground and over embankments in a wide, horizontal carpet that is outstanding for slope stabilization, bank coverage, and flowing over retaining walls. The pure white flowers (unusual among lantanas, which are predominantly yellow, orange, and purple) create a clean, bright effect that combines well with any color of companion planting — providing the extraordinary heat tolerance and butterfly attraction of lantana without the color constraints of the more common yellow-orange-red types. Like all trailing lantanas, White Lightnin' is continuously blooming from last frost through hard frost in zone 9 with no deadheading required. The sterile or nearly sterile nature of L. montevidensis reduces the invasive concerns associated with L. camara berries. Outstanding for slopes, embankments, and as a spreading groundcover in hot, dry, difficult-to-water areas where most plants fail. Gulf fritillary and monarch butterflies visit the flowers constantly throughout the long bloom season.
